title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
When you merge two hotspots, the data from two locations is combined into a single hotspot. For example, if hotspot A is plotted correctly, but hotspot B has a similar name and is plotted nearby (and is a clear duplicate), you should merge hotspot B *into* hotspot A. In almost all cases, you should also select the “Delete after merging” check box so that the hotspot you merged the data from (in this case hotspot B) will be deleted. 
Note: Please be VERY careful when merging and deleting hotspots as this action cannot be reversed. 
ALWAYS KEEP MORE REFINED LOCATIONS SEPARATE FROM THE MAIN LOCATION [e.g., DO NOT MERGE “Rocky Mountain NP–Endovalley Campground” into the “Rocky Mountain NP (Larimer Co)” hotspot].





Above: This warning appears if you attempt to Merge any hotspot that is actively part of a Hotspot Group.
crumbs: Part 1: How to Review and Edit Hotspots

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Although hotspots should generally be accessible to the public, occasional locations are not open to the public but may be regularly birded by those who have been authorized to do so. It is still appropriate to list the location as a hotspot since it is desirable to pool the data from multiple observers. 
Be sure to indicate a hotspot is Restricted Access in the Hotspot Features list. This will ensure that a “Restricted Access” tag also appears next to the hotspot name on the eBird website. Until a similar UI tag is available in eBird mobile, we recommend also placing  the words “restricted access” (or the equivalent in the local language) in lower-case in parentheses following the location name. Detailed access instructions should not be part of the hotspot name; provide these in the Plan Your Visit section of the Hotspot about page.

Examples:

Patuxent River Naval Air Station (restricted access)
Holme Bird Observatory (restricted access)
Finca Tempixque (acceso restringido)
crumbs: Part 4: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Keep Names Concise – Hotspot names should be under 100 characters, with shorter names always preferred. Brevity helps ensure clarity, ease of use, and consistency across platforms. Opt for widely recognized place names or simple descriptive terms to maintain readability whenever possible.
Avoid Emojis – Emojis should not be included in hotspot names. This is the official policy to maintain compatibility with analytical software and ensure consistency across eBird’s global dataset. We recognize the value visual indicators can bring, and we are actively exploring alternative ways to incorporate this information without affecting data integrity.
crumbs: Part 4: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Exactly where to plot a hotspot is something of a judgment call. These general rules should be followed:

Plot the hotspot in a place that is a good representation of where most birding takes place. If a 400 hectare park has a well-used hiking trail in the western third and a patch of forest with no trails that takes up the eastern two-thirds, plot the hotspot closer to the heavily used western end, not in the middle or eastern portion.
Plot the hotspot in a place that navigation apps (e.g., Google Maps, Waze) will accurately direct a birder to the location. For example, placing a hotspot in the middle of the woods that would direct Google Maps to an access point that does not exist, instead of the primary parking area, is not ideal.
For general areas (AKA “list-building parent hotspots”),  plot them in a central location relative to their constituent sub-hotspots, but make sure they are sufficiently separated from other hotspots to stand out from the rest. This helps identify the list-building parent hotspots on a map. For example, if there are a cluster of hotspots around a wildlife drive in a refuge, place the list-building parent hotspot towards the refuge’s center, sufficiently away from the other hotspots so that users can easily find it and select it.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
In addition, in some parts of the world (e.g., western USA) it is common to go birding in small towns because the trees around town provide oasis-like habitat for migrants and vagrants. In these cases, please add “town” English), “pueblo” (Spanish), or an equivalent in parentheses after the hotspot name so that it is clear that the hotspot refers to birding in town. Do not do this for larger cities and towns, since this is intended only when a town area is fairly small and is typically covered by birding the semi-urban or suburban area on foot as a discrete habitat. (Despite the guidance above, in some cases, sub-hotspots would be appropriate when considering the town as an appropriately-sized parent hotspot.)

Examples: 

Campo (town)
Punta Banda (pueblo)
Rossport (town site)

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Chemin – Ch. 
Compté – Cté
Parc Nacional – PNac
Refuge faunique or Réserve faunique – RF 
Saint or Sainte – St or Ste
Zone d’exploitation contrôlée – ZECOther abbreviations, especially in non-English languages, can be adopted by local hotspot teams. However, these need to be readily understood by the eBirding community and also need to be used consistently within the region and (ideally) outside of it. In general, use “and” instead of an ampersand (&), but in cases where hotspot names are very long, or where ampersands are already widely used (e.g., C&O Canal–Violette’s Lock), feel free to use an ampersand to reduce the length.
It is acceptable to use internationally recognized region abbreviations (which appear in URLs in eBird, such as https://ebird.org/region/MX-CHP) for countries, states, or provinces. Please use other abbreviations very sparingly. Please be careful when using abbreviations that can be confused with internationally recognized postal abbreviations (in the above list, CA (California), NA (Namibia), NM (New Mexico), and WA (Washington) are confusing exceptions given the other abbreviations we allow). Do not abbreviate the full name of a national park or wildlife area (For example, please use Rocky Mountain NP, not RMNP). When in doubt, spell it out.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
The parent hotspot should be named for the area that encompasses the entire group. “General” or “general area” may be appended to the Parent hotspot name if there is the potential for confusion between the parent hotspot (referring to checklists spanning multiple sites or the entire area) and any specific, individual sub-hotspot.
Any modifiers or warnings that are specific to the parent location alone (e.g., “Use more precise location”) should be added in parentheses.



List-building parent hotspots represent very large areas that are too spatially imprecise to be useful for most scientific analyses. In Hotspot Groups, List-building parent hotspots are automatically identified for any group with a bounding perimeter area of >80km2.
For very large list-building hotspots, we recommend including a parenthetical note that discourages casual or regular use of these Hotspots and encourages more specific birding—e.g., “(use more precise loc.)”—whenever there is space to use it. Our recommendation is to keep this as short and direct as possible. 
 Any list-building parent hotspot that is considered problematic for data entry should include an asterisk after the core part of the name. An asterisk following the name is also meant to be a warning to savvy eBirders not to pick this spot if they are willing and able to submit their checklist at a more precise location. The asterisk may also help to ensure that scientific data users don’t treat these as site-specific checklists. We ask that asterisks not be used anywhere else in hotspots so that they can be specifically used to designate these special types of locations. 
The below hotspots use our recommended names:

Glacier NP* general (use more precise loc.)
Khao Yai NP* general (use more precise loc.)
Maasai Mara NR* general (use more precise loc.)
Cerro Punta* general area (use more precise loc.)
Akatau Mountains* general area (use more precise loc.)




Sub-hotspots should follow the parent hotspot separated by a double hyphen (–) without spaces on either side. The correct structure is: [Parent Hotspot]–[sub-hotspot]. For example, “Montezuma NWR–Towpath Rd.”

The following variations are INCORRECT:

Spaces on either side of the dashes: “Montezuma NWR – Towpath Rd.”
Single dash: “Montezuma NWR-Towpath Rd.” 
Emdash: Montezuma NWR—Towpath Rd.” (A common autocorrect change to watch out for!)

NOTE: Using the correct structure here has been critically important since 2021, since this naming structure is used in eBird Mobile to recommend specific hotspots to the user.

Examples:

Khao Yai NP–headquarters vicinity
Khao Yai NP–Nong Phak Chi
Yosemite NP–Tuolumne Meadows
Yosemite NP–Glacier Pt.
Reserva de la Biosfera El Cielo–Senda Alta Cima
Reserva de la Biosfera El Cielo–Rancho del Cielo


Do not make a city or town a parent hotspot with sub-hotspots. When it is helpful to identify a location by city or town, please use this format: [Location], [City or Town Name]. Furthermore, large cities do not ever need to be specified in the location name (except when two hotspots with the same name might be confused). For example, “Garden of the Gods Park, Colorado Springs” is undesirable; it should be named “Garden of the Gods Park” and the mapped location will indicate where it is more specifically. If a city, town, or township is used with a road name it should generally follow the road name. For instance, “Butler Rd., Olmito” rather than “Olmito–Butler Rd.”
Entire “Birding Trails”, covering a large section of a state, should never have sub-hotspots. In the great majority of birding trails, each site or stop on a birding trail should have its own location, either as a hotspot or a sub-hotspots within another entity. It is permissible to have a birding trail site number in parentheses after the name, for example, Laguna Atascosa NWR (LTC 024).



In cases where the parent location name is long, abbreviations can be established for sub-hotspots, provided the parent hotspot uses the full, unabbreviated version. For example, in Figure 2 above, Cape Island–SCMM–Cove Pool is allowed because SCMM is defined (as South Cape May Meadows) in the nearby parent hotspot.
crumbs: Part 4: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
The eBird Basic Dataset (EBD) and Sampling Event Dataset (SED) provide data at the observation and checklist level, respectively. Because each checklist is associated with one specific location, Hotspot groups don’t affect how data appears in the EBD and SED. 
The location information you’ll see depends on where the checklist was submitted:

Checklist submitted to parent hotspot: The ‘LOCALITY’ and ‘LOCALITY ID’ fields show the parent hotspot name and ID
Checklist submitted to sub-hotspot in group: The ‘LOCALITY’ and ‘LOCALITY ID’ fields show only that specific sub-hotspot—no parent or group information is included

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
A single out-of-range rarity often generates a large amount of attention from birders. When such a bird occurs at a new site (i.e., not at an already-established hotspot) it is good practice to quickly set up a “stakeout” hotspot to give eBirders an accurate and consistent location to use when they visit. Please encourage those who “chase” these rarities to use the hotspot rather than creating their own location. These hotspots should be retained even after the bird has departed so that new eBird users can enter past rarities.
Our conventions for “stakeout hotspots” are:

Plot the point as precisely as possible in relation to where the rarity is being seen
Always begin with “stakeout” in lowercase 
Next, include the common name of the bird in the local language
Next, include a geographical identifier; this can be the town, a well-known road, or other feature that will distinguish this hotspot from others for the same species in the region. 
Do not include county, state, or country, since these are automatically added by eBird. 
Finally, include the date range when the bird was seen; for ongoing rarities, these names may need to be edited in real time if the rare bird persists for months or years



Examples:

stakeout Black-throated Gray Warbler, 103 James H. Luther Dr., Taunton (Jan 2013)
stakeout Black-throated Sparrow, Elora (2005-06)
stakeout Eurasian Blackcap, Durbanville (Jul 2017)
crumbs: Part 4: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Avenue – Ave.
Conservation Area – CA
County – Co.
Department – Dept.
Highway – Hwy.

United States Highway 6 – US-6
Colorado Highway 8 – CO-8
Interstate Highway 90 – I-90
County Road 18 – CR-18

Important Bird Area – IBA
Kilometer – km
Mile marks – mm (e.g., I-90–mm92)
Mountain or Mount – Mt.
National Historic Site – NHS
National Park – NP 
National Wildlife Refuge – NWR
Natural Area – NA
National Monument – NM
Point – Pt. (do not use this for Port)
Road – Rd. 
Saint – St
State Park – SP
Street – St.
Township – Twp.
Wildlife Area – WA
Wildlife Management Area – WMA
Wastewater Treatment Plant, Water Treatment Plant, or Sewage Plant – WTP

*Special note for Wastewater Treatment Plants, which may have different terminology in different regions. Water Treatment Plant, Sewage Ponds, Sewage Lagoons, Sewage Treatment Plant etc. all refer to basically the same type of site (which of course are almost always hot spots for birds!). In the interest of standardization, we recommend using WTP wherever possible. If a certain site is widely known as Sewage Treatment Plant and using WTP would create confusion, it is permissible to spell those out; do not use STP or WWTP.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
A Hotspot Group consists of two or more hotspots that are part of the same continuous geographical area.
Hotspot Groups organize multiple hotspots within a larger area. For example, a state park or wildlife refuge might contain separate eBird hotspots for individual trails, viewpoints, or other popular birding spots. These hotspots would collectively form a hotspot group under a single overview page (AKA parent hotspot) that refers to the entire area.
Each hotspot can only belong to one group at a time due to technical requirements related to data aggregation.
Hotspots should only be grouped if they occupy the same general area—typically this area has clearly defined boundaries and/or is under a single jurisdiction or management entity (i.e., sites should generally form a contiguous cluster). Birders often may visit multiple hotspots within this boundary on a single visit (ideally, but not always, keeping separate checklists). 


The following should NOT be made into hotspot groups:

Long linear routes (long trails, river stretches, highways, etc.) unless they are part of a single administrative unit (e.g., Buffalo National River is a single national park) 
Large cities (small towns under 7 sq mi / 20 sq km are OK)  
Many isolated or non-contiguous properties, even if under the same managing organization.




Yes! See our guidance for Hotspot Groups that cross administrative boundaries below. 
When a hotspot group crosses county/state/district/province lines, we recommend creating a “regional” hotspot on each side of the border. Include the respective administrative area in parentheses at the end of the hotspot name—e.g., “Blue Lake (Lewis Co.)” and “Blue Lake (Cowlitz Co.)”

When a hotspot group crosses county/state/district/province lines, we recommend creating a “regional” hotspot on each side of the border. Include the respective administrative area in parentheses at the end of the hotspot name—e.g., “Blue Lake (Lewis Co.)” and “Blue Lake (Cowlitz Co.)”

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Follow Hotspot Naming policies. Parent locations should have “general” or “general area” in the name if there could be confusion between a precise site and the overarching area. 
Sub-hotspots do not HAVE to include the parent location in the name. In other words, you can form Hotspots groups where sub-hotspots follow mixed naming practices, some with the parent location in the name and some without. 
Sub-hotspots SHOULD have the parent location at the beginning of their name, followed by double-dash (–) and the sub-hotspot descriptor unless the sub-hotspot is an obvious and well-known standalone location. 
Including the parent location in the subhotspot name is helpful to ensure hotspots from the same area are grouped together in dropdown lists and search results.

Hotspot CSV files can also be converted into Google Earth (kml) files. After downloading the Hotspot CSV, open the file in Excel or similar software. Insert a new, empty row 1. Enter the following column headings into your new row 1: 

LocalityID
Country
Subnat1
Subnat2
Latitude
Longitude
Name
EditDate
Species

Only Latitude (column E), Longitude (column F), and Name (column G) are required for Google Earth. The remaining columns can be deleted if you do not plan to use them in your KML file. 
Save the updated file in CSV or XLSX format. Then, use a KML converter—such as http://www.earthpoint.us/ExcelToKml.aspx—to prepare the file for Google Earth. 
Instead of a KML converter, you can create a new personal map on Google Maps (https://www.google.com/maps/d/), import your formatted CSV or XLSX file, and then export it as KML/KMZ.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Downloadable and machine-readable files can be accessed from the eBird Hotspot API. Hotspot editors will find it useful to download a CSV file of hotspots in their region. 
To obtain a CSV file of eBird Hotspots in a region, first request an eBird API key here: https://ebird.org/data/download (your personal API key is unique to you, please do not share it!) 
You will also need the appropriate region code. These can be found in the URLs of the Region Explorer at https://ebird.org/explore. For example, the region code for Zimbabwe (https://ebird.org/region/ZW) is ZW. The region code for Cork, Munster, Ireland (https://ebird.org/region/IE-M-CK) is IE-M-CK. 
Substitute your API key and appropriate region code for {APIKEY} and {REGIONCODE} in the following web address:

https://api.ebird.org/v2/ref/hotspot/{REGIONCODE}?fmt=csv&key={APIKEY}

Copy and paste the web address into your browser. The result will be a downloaded CSV file of all eBird Hotspots in that region. 
Example: If your API key was 1a2b3c4d5e and your regions of interest were Zimbabwe (ZW) and Cork, Munster, Ireland (IE-M-CK), you would paste the following URLs into your web browser:

https://api.ebird.org/v2/ref/hotspot/ZW?fmt=csv&key=1a2b3c4d5e
https://api.ebird.org/v2/ref/hotspot/IE-M-CK?fmt=csv&key=1a2b3c4d5e

The eBird API can also provide a list of species seen within the last 1-30 days from any hotspot, which can then be embedded on other websites. Our APIs are fully documented here, and most of these APIs are freely available for non-profit use; commercial use of the Hotspot API (or any other eBird APIs) is not permitted.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Many hotspots cannot be immediately accepted, or accepted at all. Here are some common situations.

If a user recommends a hotspot that clearly does not meet the criteria for a shared hotspot (such as “My Backyard” or just the user’s name such as “Steve Jones”), go ahead and resolve the recommendation by clicking “Review” and then “Revert to Personal Location.”
Be conscious of duplicate suggestions. Sometimes, a user will recommend a hotspot similar to one that already exists. In that case, don’t accept the Hotspot. Consider merging the suggested hotspot. If the suggestion is an improvement over the existing hotspot, merge the suggestion, then move or rename the existing hotspot.
Occasionally, a hotspot will be recommended that has no data associated with it. In these cases the hotspot editing screen will have a delete option next to the hotspot name. This only appears for locations with no data associated with them (i.e., you cannot delete a location that has associated data). In these cases, feel free to delete the location if it is not a worthy hotspot. Some hotspots might appear to have no data, but they won’t have the deletion option. This is most likely because they have hidden checklists or only have sensitive, rare and/or unaccepted species, but they still have data, so deletion isn’t an option.
If you just can’t figure out what the user’s intent was, send an email to find out. When in doubt, revert to a personal location. This is always the safest option.


Hotspot organization and hierarchy can be managed in additional ways, so decisions to accept new hotspots should focus on their potential benefit to data entry and aggregation. Read the sections on What is a Hotspot? and Hotspot Naming for guidance on special circumstances such as sub-hotspots or list-building parent hotspots.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Which language is displayed first?

If a user has the eBird website or their browser set to a particular language, we first display About content in that language, if available.
If no content is available in the user’s browser/web language, we then try to display content in the regional default language, if applicable. (e.g., For eBird Japan, the regional default language is Japanese. For eBird Chile, the regional default language is Spanish)
If there is no content in either the user’s preferred language or the regional default language, we will show ANY available content.* 
Finally, if there is no content at all, we display a message “Nothing added yet for this hotspot. Suggest content”
Note that bird names that use the species tags will always display for logged-in users according to the user’s common name preferences, regardless of the other language settings.

* Whenever multiple languages are available, go to Edit Content > Manage Languages to choose which language is displayed first. 





If a language has no content: Go to Edit Content > Manage Languages to delete it. (Be careful, this will also delete any existing content!)

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Thank you for your help managing hotspots!
Team eBird: Marshall Iliff, Jenna Curtis, Chris Wood, Ian Davies, and John Garrett
and the 
eBird Hotspot Management Team: Ken Ostermiller, Ben Cacace, Daniel Scheiman, Jane Tatchell, and Rob Bielawski (with special thanks to Ezra James for help with the 2025 version of this document)

title: Introduction
text:
Thank you for volunteering to help review and manage eBird hotspots. This document describes the purpose of hotspots, how to review and approve requests for new hotspots, and how to rename, relocate, merge and manage the hierarchy of existing hotspots.
Hotspots allow birders to submit data to common locations. The idea is to aggregate data from these hotspots and provide output that is useful to birders wishing to learn more about birds and their distribution. With pooled data from multiple observers, hotspots become a valuable tool for finding out when and where to see birds in your area and elsewhere. All eBird hotspots appear in the eBird Hotspot Explorer (http://ebird.org/ebird/hotspots).
It is important to remember that data collected at FINE resolutions are much more valuable than data aggregated over very large areas. For this reason, we try to include sub-hotpots (see Hotspot Definitions and Hierarchy) in order to incentivize birders towards birding across shorter distances and using hotspots that are closely tied to their location on the ground.  So, while we do have a hotspot for Rocky Mountain NP (Larimer Co), we also have many hotspots for locations within Rocky Mountain NP, for example Rocky Mountain NP–Endovalley Picnic Area.


eBird hotspots are primarily useful as a way of aggregating data from multiple observers under a single location. This simplifies data entry by providing curated, properly-sited, and sensibly-named locations that represent a given birding site and aggregates similar birding effort under a single location. Because Hotspots can significantly impact the data entry process—influencing where birders choose to go both within a site and within a region—they should be created, named, and positioned to clearly and intuitively reflect the area they cover. Most hotspots should be publicly accessible, but see Restricted Access locations section below. 
During data entry in eBird mobile, users that start a checklist will get a list of recommended hotspots nearby. Submitted data can then easily be explored on eBird hotspot pages on the web, which includes tools like bar charts, iconic birds, illustrated checklists, photo galleries, recent visits, and of course an official list of species for the site. Hotspot summary pages can be really useful for landowners or land managers as a quick way to understand birds on the property, but see below section on Hotspot Definitions and Hierarchy and note that hotspots should not be merged just to improve data summaries (data can be combined for data summaries in other ways!). 
Please think of eBird hotspots as curated sites visited repeatedly by the eBird community.
eBird hotspots are not necessarily superlative birding locations; they can be designated for high species diversity, presence of one or several specialty species, a regularly birded location that is publicly accessible, or almost anywhere in which it is desirable to aggregate data from multiple eBirders in a single point. Sometimes hotspots represent frequently visited tourist sites where birders are likely to keep an eBird checklist, especially if visiting from abroad and experiencing new life birds. In certain cases, eBird hotspots are established for scientific surveys. Some examples of hotspots that may not be obvious include:

Piazza San Pietro [urban, paved plaza, and popular tourist site]
Sap Anan–PTT gas station on Hwy. 4 (southbound) [gas station and rest area along route popular with birders; not particularly good for birds but a spot many birders will stretch their legs and take a break]
Detroit Metro Airport (restricted access) [this and many airports are appropriate hotspots, even when viewing from the terminals only; many traveling birders will enter lists and airports do provide grassland habitat that may attract specialty species]




Examples of sites that should not be hotspots include:

Personal yards and other private property
Personal walking routes
Property that is visited only by one person or by small numbers of people with permission only
Random interesting observations unlikely to be repeated (e.g., along a major road or at an unremarkable area of urban or suburban habitat)

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
This section refers to linear features that are too long to be represented by a single hotspot except for historical records and getting personal life lists up to date)  This would not apply to linear features within discrete, non-linear hotspots, such as trails within parks. It also does not apply to pelagic transects (see the Pelagic Hotspot section below).
Linear routes (e.g., rivers, roads, and hiking trails)—while not eligible to be Hotspot groups—can still consist of a hierarchy of both stationary and point-to-point “sub-hotspots” (term used loosely, not in the technical sense of a Hotspot group) that follow a hierarchical naming structure. 
One example of a linear route  consisting of multiple “sub-hotspots” is  the Alaska Highway: Alaska Hwy–Yukon River bridge to M’Clintock Bay (point-to-point hotspot) and Alaska Hwy–Drury’s Farm pond (stationary hotspot). 
For point-to-point sub-hotspots, the naming convention is [Parent Hospot]–[Point A] to [Point B].

Buffalo National River–Tyler Bend to Gilbert
Alakai Swamp Trail–Trailhead to Pihea Trail Junction
Carretera 175/Oaxaca-Tuxtepec–Km 90-95

Place the marker roughly equidistant between the two points. Not every stretch of linear hotspot may qualify as a sub-hotspot. Use your judgment and consider habitats and the history of how a given stretch has been birded.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Parent locations aggregate data across all hotspots in the group. Statistics for a parent location represent the entire group’s combined data, including public checklists submitted to the parent.
The parent hotspot page functions as the group overview, summarizing checklist submission across all hotspots in the group. Likewise, the leaderboard shows top contributors based on combined activity across all hotspots.
This aggregation encourages greater location precision. You can now submit separate checklists to each hotspot and still have your activity count toward the cumulative Top 100 for the entire area.
This technical framework may also allow us to eventually aggregate personal locations into hotspot summaries in the future (but not any time soon).
Note on Bar Charts: Bar Charts for the parent location will display aggregated frequency data, histograms, and chart data for the entire group. To obtain Bar Chart data for the parent hotspot ONLY, add &aggregateHotspots=false to the end of the Bar Chart web address.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Most hotspots are discrete, continuous sites that accurately represent a geographical region—often defined by land ownership or conservation status. Typical hotspots can also be thought of as representing birder behavior within those properties, including a walking or driving path (i.e., track) and typical strategies for looking for birds. One could theoretically draw a boundary to represent any hotspot (this functionality might be added to eBird in the future to more formally define these areas).
Most hotspots should be coverable in a discrete birding event, most typically either 1) stationary count at a certain vantage point (e.g., Shaumta Raptor Watchpoint); 2) a traveling count described by a long (e.g., Reserva Ecológica Costanera Sur) or short (e.g., Brooklyn Bridge Park) linear, out-and-back, looped, or meandering walking route; 3) or a traveling count involving short legs by car, bike, or foot that is broken up by stops at key locations or vantage points, such as along a wildlife drive (e.g., Edwin B. Forsythe NWR–Wildlife Drive or Carretero Satipo).
Many hotspots represent a single, discrete, stand-alone site. However, in some cases there are many hotspots in a small area, and within those areas there may be discrete hierarchy of hotspots that may include a parent hotspot, several sub-hotspots, and occasionally an overarching “grandparent” hotspot (see Hotspot Groups)
As a general rule, Hotspot Groups should contain sites that are all part of the same management unit, such as a state park, a national wildlife refuge, or a city park. A hierarchical naming structure can also be used even when sites are not part of an “official” Hotspot Group, such as along particularly good sections of birding roads, rivers, or trails (see Linear Routes below). 

Fig. 1. Cape May Point area, showing the large number of hotspots (red, orange, yellow, and blue pins) south of the Cape May Canal, which runs through the middle of this image.
crumbs: Part 4: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots

Forsythe NWR–Wildlife Drive or Carretero Satipo).
Many hotspots represent a single, discrete, stand-alone site. However, in some cases there are many hotspots in a small area, and within those areas there may be discrete hierarchy of hotspots that may include a parent hotspot, several sub-hotspots, and occasionally an overarching “grandparent” hotspot (see Hotspot Groups)
As a general rule, Hotspot Groups should contain sites that are all part of the same management unit, such as a state park, a national wildlife refuge, or a city park. A hierarchical naming structure can also be used even when sites are not part of an “official” Hotspot Group, such as along particularly good sections of birding roads, rivers, or trails (see Linear Routes below). 

Fig. 1. Cape May Point area, showing the large number of hotspots (red, orange, yellow, and blue pins) south of the Cape May Canal, which runs through the middle of this image.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
This section describes how to sign in to the Hotspot Review Tools, view hotspot suggestions in your area, open and review a hotspot suggestion, and then accept (or reject) a hotspot. Chrome, Safari, and Mozilla Firefox browsers are all fully supported; other browsers may not work as well. 

Log in to the eBird Hotspot Review Tools (https://ebird.org/admin/hotspots.htm) using your standard eBird credentials. If you are logged in to a different account, you will not be able to edit hotspots. We recommend bookmarking this page.
Make sure that you are in the Hotspots tab at the top left of the screen (see below).


View and Sort Pending Hotspot Suggestions
The list of hotspots waiting for evaluation in your review region is visible on the default page of the Hotspot Review Tools: https://ebird.org/admin/hotspots.htm. You can sort this page by any of the columns; just click the header (e.g., Name, Country), and you’ll be all set. You can also search the list of pending hotspots by using Command+F on a Mac, or Ctrl+F on a PC. 
Open and Review Hotspot Suggestions





It is important to know that clicking “Review” immediately accepts a given hotspot, and then allows editing (changing name, location, or merging) to improve it as needed. If it is not an appropriate hotspot, there are just two clicks required: “Review” and then “Revert to personal location” and then the hotspot request is removed and the hotspot will not be created. This tentative acceptance makes the process efficient, but it is important to understand. 
Note: For more details and explanations on editing hotspots (including deleting a hotspot and reverting a hotspot to a personal location) refer to the “Editing Existing Hotspots” section.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text: When in doubt, follow the grammar rules of the local language. In English, eBird hotspots can be considered proper names and should generally be capitalized, including all adjectives, common nouns, and the definite article. Most formal geographic names have two parts, denoting the specific and the generic (e.g., Amherst Marsh). The generic part (“Marsh”, in this example) tells the kind of place, feature, or area to which the name refers, and the specific part (“Amherst”) uniquely identifies the particular place, feature, or area.  Exceptions include articles and prepositions (e.g., Alto de la Cruz, Lake of the Ozarks, Fond du Lac) and phrases that give relative geographic reference  (e.g., Enders Reservoir–below dam, Dartmoor NP–east of Tavistock.)

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
We recommend placing the Parent hotspot at a central location away from more precisely placed hotspots. This helps to reduce site selection confusion and ensures eBird Mobile’s “Recommended Location” algorithm only suggests the parent hotspot when a GPS track spans multiple sub-hotspots or covers the broader area. 
If the group includes two or more regions (counties, states/provinces, or countries) place the Parent hotspot in the region with the most group hotspots and checklist activity. 
See also: Plotting Hotspots

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Occasionally, a hotspot name will need an additional modifier to clarify the location. The most common situations are lakes and roads that cross county, department, state, province, or even country borders (as shown in the examples below) or common place names that may appear more than once in a state or province (e.g., there may be more than one Blue Lake). Such modifiers should follow the parent hotspot name and the sub-hotspot name (if present), can use “Co.” to abbreviate County, and should always appear in parentheses. These modifiers should be used sparingly.

Examples: 

Black Butte Lake (Glenn Co.) and Black Butte Lake (Tehama Co.)
Boca Chica Beach (LTC 043)–USA side and Boca Chica (Tamaulipas)
Balsas–Rio Marañon (Dept. Amazonas) and Balsas–Rio Marañón (Dept. Cajamarca)


Other modifiers may sometimes be useful to clarify a location, such as the town, especially when there are confusingly similar names. We generally recommend using a comma to add the name of the town.

Examples:

Nauset Light Beach, Eastham
Nauset Beach, Orleans


Hotspots that cover properties managed by land trusts and other land preservation agencies may have specific organizational attribution by adding the name or acronym in parentheses after the name of the property, for example Crooked Creek Preserve (TNC) for a property named Crooked Creek Preserve that is managed by The Nature Conservancy. Since the names of Land Trusts can be quite long, we generally recommend using these abbreviations rather than the full name of the Land Trust. Although the meaning here may not be immediately obvious to all users, it often is meaningful locally and is something that anyone can look up on a search engine.
We do not ever recommend using latitude and longitude in the hotspot name; the coordinates of a hotspot are always officially defined by where the hotspot is sited and all hotspots in eBird are associated with a specific latitude and longitude. Adding these to the name is unnecessary and likely would lead to errors if the hotspot ever were moved but the name not also adjusted.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
It has become increasingly popular to watch birdcams (or other remote-sensing technology) and keep lists in eBird. This practice is not formally endorsed by eBird (see our policy here), but since these checklists may be treated as Public and may have public records, it is preferable to use established hotspot locations when this practice is being adopted by multiple people to avoid too much location scatter. Please get in touch with eBird Central for approval of any such hotspots. We recommend including the overarching hotspot/area name, followed by a double-dash and “feedercam” or “trailcam,” to make sure these are readily identifiable; the name in parentheses is the official name of the camera. Please do not add the camera weblink.

Examples: 

Canopy Lodge–feedercam only (Panama Fruit Feeder Cam)
Manitouwadge–feedercam only (Ontario FeederWatch Cam)

text: On rare occasions a rarity may appear on private land but access will be controlled so as to respect the wishes of the property owner. In such cases, the stakeout hotspot should be plotted some distance away from the actual location in a public area and ideally the name should indicate that the location plot is intentionally inaccurate and that the access is restricted. Ideally, once the bird of interest has departed, the hotspot can be moved to the correct location.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
In Hotspot Groups, the Parent hotspot represents the entire area covered by the group. However, some areas are so large that the Parent hotspot carries too much spatial uncertainty to be useful for scientific analyses. 
For example, Yellowstone National Park covers 13,000 square miles (33,670 square km) and spans four US states. Any checklist assigned to the “Yellowstone National Park (general area)” hotspot lacks the spatial precision needed for eBird Status and Trends models.
Currently, when a traveling checklist covers a distance of 50 mi (80 km) or more, we automatically mark it Not Public. 
When the area within the bounding box of the group covers 80 km2 or more…
The Parent hotspot will not appear as a Recommended Location for eBird Mobile checklists and cannot be selected from the map when submitting eBird Mobile checklists (the parent location will still appear on eBird Mobile Explore). 
We recommend marking any checklist submitted to a List-building Parent hotspot ‘Not Public’ with the reason “Checklist-List Building”

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
eBird is a global project and we recommend that site names be listed in the local language (e.g., Spanish for Central America, Portuguese for Portugal, French for France and Quebec, German for Germany, etc.). These names also should generally match the names used on maps and in local signage. 
However, when the local language uses an alphabet that is not easily read by English-speakers (or other Germanic/Romance languages), please list the site name in the local language first, followed by an English translation in parentheses. See examples below:

Taiwan: 宜蘭–頂寮生態公園 (Yilan–Dingliao Ecological Park) http://ebird.org/ebird/hotspot/L3291680
Japan: 大田区–東京港野鳥公園 (Ota Ward–Tokyo Port Wild Bird Park) http://ebird.org/ebird/hotspot/L927941

Note: Please use accents in non-English words in accordance with correct spelling in the original language (e.g., Río Guadalupe, Parque San José, Cañon San Pedro). Please DO NOT use accents over capital letters.

title: Part 4: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots
text:
When approving new hotspots and reviewing current hotspots, try to use names that conform to the naming conventions described below. Consistent naming helps users find the correct hotspot when using the mapping tool to submit a checklist, as well as when selecting hotspots from drop-down lists. Searching for output based on hotspot names is also easier when the naming conforms to consistent standards.
It’s also helpful to keep in mind how eBird uses and displays hotspot names. For example, most eBird output (e.g., Hotspot Explorer, Region Explorer) includes the hotspots in geographical context, and when the name of the site is listed, it includes the county, state, and country. Therefore, it is not necessary to include the name of the county, state, province, or country in the hotspot name unless it is needed for clarity. 
Hotspot names should generally use the formal, official name of the park, forest, wildlife area, or some other geographic identifier—if there is one. It is generally not good practice to name hotspots for birds that might be seen there or for habitat that may not persist over time, although there may be sites for specific species that do merit a hotspot named for the species of bird. A notable exception is for “stakeout” rarities, which have a special section (Stakeout Hotspots) below.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Existing hotspots can be renamed, moved, merged, deleted, and reverted to personal locations using the Hotspot Explorer in eBird. 

Sign in to eBird.
Navigate to the hotspot you wish to edit. Easy ways to do that include:

Click the hotspot name from an eBird checklist.
Find it on the eBird Hotspot map (https://ebird.org/hotspots). 
Select it from the list of hotspots on a region page (top 100 hotspots only).

From the Hotspot map: Click the Edit link to open the Edit Location page.
From an individual Hotspot page: Tap Admin from the navigation sidebar











Rename—To change the hotspot name, select the Rename option, type the new name, and click Rename. (See Part IV: Standards and Naming Conventions for eBird Hotspots)

Manage Sub-Hotspots—see Hotspot Groups
Move—To move the hotspot, select the Move option, click on the map to plot the new location, and then click Move again. (See Plotting Hotspots) NOTE: If you know the coordinates of the new location, enter them in the Zoom to: box, press the <Enter> key, and then click Move.
Merge—See Merging Hotspots. To merge two hotspots, select the Merge option. The map will display the surrounding hotspots as red pins, and the hotspot you’re working with will be shown as an /orange pin with a plus sign. Click the hotspot that you want to merge your current hotspot *INTO*, select the Delete after merging check box, and then click Merge. Be careful! Merging cannot be undone. NOTE: If you do not select Delete after merging, the old hotspot will persist with no data.

Revert to Personal Location—Reverts a hotspot to a personal location. If the personal location that is created is shared among several observers, the one with the oldest checklist (i.e., the first checklist ever submitted for the site, not the oldest observation date) will become the “owner” of the location and is  responsible for any moves, renaming, or merging of this location, and also is the only one able to recommend it again as a hotspot. NOTE: You can still find these in the “Reviewed” tab if you want to reverse your decision.





Above: You will not be able to revert a hotspot to a personal location if it is part of a Hotspot Group. 
Delete—You do not have the option to delete a hotspot unless it has no data associated with it. In this case, a blue delete option appears next to the name. There is little point in maintaining hotspots with no data, unless you are confident they will be useful in the future. Please delete these if you deem it appropriate.

There is little point in maintaining hotspots with no data, unless you are confident they will be useful in the future. Please delete these if you deem it appropriate.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Yes! Hotspot groups can cross geopolitical boundaries. This does not mean an individual checklist will appear in multiple counties. 



Parent hotspots display checklist activity from all sub-locations in the group. If the group covers multiple regions, data from multiple counties could appear on the parent hotspot page. This does NOT feed into greater county, state/province, or country-level data. Each hotspot counts toward only one county, one state/province, and one country’s birding activity. Individual hotspots only contribute data to the regions where they are located.

In the example Hotspot Group above, only checklists submitted to hotspots in Region A or submitted directly to the parent location will appear in Region A stats. Checklists from hotspots in Region B will also appear on the parent location page, but only count towards Region B stats. 



We understand that reviewers may hesitate to create groups where the parent hotspot aggregates data from multiple regions, as it could inadvertently lead to data from one county being submitted to a parent location in another county. If you have concerns about this, please see our guidance below. 



For hotspot groups that cross borders because they cover large areas (e.g., national parks, wildlife refuges), we are implementing a special “large area process”: any hotspot group with a bounding area greater than 80km2 will be designated a List Building Location (see above). 



Hotspot groups <80km2 can still span multiple geopolitical regions. Common examples include bridges or dams that cross borders along rivers. 
When the same habitats exist on both sides of a geopolitical border (e.g., a road or arbitrary line cutting through a forest), the birds found on either side should be very similar—it would be hard to tell from a single checklist which side of the border the observer was on if you didn’t know the exact location. 
Conversely, when geopolitical boundaries follow strong ecological boundaries (e.g., Pima and Santa Cruz counties in Madera Canyon) the species lists of two hotspots just across the border from each other might be dramatically different—it’s easy to tell which side a checklist came from. 
When people submit checklists directly to the parent hotspot of a group, their observations will “roll up” into the county, state/province, and country where the parent hotspot is located. (Note: it is almost never best practice to submit to a parent hotspot, since finer-scale locations are always preferable)
So, if the main goal is maintaining accurate regional data, the key question is: will creating a Hotspot Group (under 80km²) clearly disrupt regional data where the parent hotspot is located? 




Do most birds cross freely from one side of the border to the other?
Could you tell which side of the border a checklist was from simply by looking at the species list and counts? 
Do the habitat conditions found on one side also exist in the other county, and vice versa?
Are species lists, high counts, and arrival/departure dates generally similar on each side of the border?

If you answered “Yes” to these questions—assuming all other hotspot group criteria are met—then a multi-region hotspot group shouldn’t cause meaningful regional data problems. (Very long traveling checklists can still cause regional data problems, so continue to be vigilant for those!)

text: Entire “Birding Trails”—multiple sites across a large region not all along the same road—should never be grouped or have sub-hotspots. In the great majority of birding trails, each site or stop on a birding trail should have its own location, either as a standalone hotspot or a sub-hotspot within a local group.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
It is not encouraged to use hotspots for pelagic birding, since the eBird best practice is to follow the eBird Pelagic Protocol and plot locations based on the exact route you traveled, ideally using tracks in eBird mobile. But there are two scenarios where hotspots have been established and these can be useful for aggregating data, especially when the data were not collected with rigorous tracks of GPS location services.

General pelagic locations: Like other list-building parent hotspots , these are most useful for aggregation of historical data under a single hotspot that is reasonably well sited. They should be named according to conventions described above for list-building parent hotspots and should only be used when established boat trips regularly visit roughly the same waters.


Examples:

Kaikoura–offshore–Albatross Encounter pelagic (general)
Tofino pelagic trip



Specific pelagic locations: Sometimes an underwater feature (e.g., canyon, seamount, shelf, bank) or other specific habitat is a specific area that is regularly targeted for pelagic birding. These can be appropriate hotspots, but of course should only be used for observations in the immediate vicinity of these features; since this is hard to define for a birder on a boat, these hotspots should only be established with good reason and should always be used with great care. Otherwise, see above for conventions.


Examples:

Hudson Canyon (Atlantic Ocean)
Escollo Gorda

text:
For pending (un-reviewed) suggestions, tap “Details” to see what changed

Accept: anything in GREEN will be added to the Hotspot About page
Decline: anything in RED will be removed from the Hotspot About page
Close: hide details without acting on them

WARNING: “Details” shows how the suggested changes will impact the current About page. If the underlying About content has changed since the suggestion was made, the comparison shows what will actually change (not necessarily what the suggester saw at the time of their revisions). 
If you Accept more recent pending suggestions, earlier pending suggestions may no longer apply (i.e., the text being changed may no longer exist on the page) We recommend quickly scanning the Details of all pending suggestions for a single hotspot before Accepting any of them.
(!) eBird Hotspot Editors can see the personal account details for the eBirder who made the suggestion, including contact details. People with content editing permissions can only see the name of the person who made the suggestion, with a link to their eBird profile if public. 





Switch between suggestions for a single hotspot and all hotspots. You can also filter pending suggestions by language

text: Hotspot review policies, instructions, and standards

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Check for duplicates—Select the Merge option. This does not merge the hotspot yet, but loads other hotspots in the area so that you can check if the hotspot you just accepted already exists nearby with the same or a similar name. If it does already exist, merge the new location with the existing hotspot. If it does not already exist, proceed with the next steps. Caution: Make sure the new hotspot is really a duplicate before merging. Merging cannot be reversed! **NOTE: When merging with an existing hotspot if you do not select Delete after merging, the new hotspot will persist with no data.
Consider if the hotspot is acceptable—see “To Accept or Not Accept”
Check that the name matches eBird naming protocols (see Part IV). If necessary, click Rename andrename the hotspot.
Confirm location—If you need to move the new hotspot, select the Move option, click the new location on the map, and then click Move again. **NOTE: This step should be last, because it automatically closes the hotspot editing window.
Consider hotspot hierarchy–if the new hotspot is best understood as a subhotspot within a group that already exists, go to the parent hotspot Admin tools and add the new one as a subhotspot. Similarly, for new parent hotspots, add any subhotspots to them.

Finito! You’re done, and another hotspot has joined the system!!! Return to the Hotspot Review Tools, and the suggestion no longer appears in the unreviewed hotspots page. 

**NOTE: If you change your mind on acceptance or need to revisit the hotspot, see the Viewing Reviewed Hotspots section below.
Allow time for the database to update. The hotspot page will be viewable with the correct link immediately, and checklists will correctly link to it, but some of the exploration tools in the Hotspot Explorer (including the map) update once every ~24 hours. 
If someone suggests the same Hotspot more than once, you will need to wait until the next day to review the others if you want to merge them. Likewise, if two users suggest the same location, review and accept one, then wait until the next day to review and merge the other.

Pro Tip: If you accidentally accept two duplicate hotspots on the same day, add “(Merge)” to the name of one so you can easily find and correct it later. 
Submit Data and Hotspot editing maps are nearly live queries (every two hours), so the hotspot lists here will be almost current.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
For instructions on About This Hotspot text sections and Features, visit the eBird help center: eBird Hotspot About Pages
A note to editors on using AI to generate Hotspot content:
AI can be a helpful tool to organize, format, and copyedit About content. However, AI has its limitations and should always be read and human-verified before publishing. 
When considering using AI for Hotspot content, we first strongly recommend generating content for the Hotspots that you are the MOST familiar with. Review the content, noting any inaccuracies. If possible, ask the AI for its sources and consult them directly to understand where content is obtained and how accurately it has been adapted. Repeat until you feel you have a good sense of the AI tool’s performance. 

Always read the content word-for-word 
If you are not familiar with a site, try to find someone who is familiar with it to review its content (consider: if the AI output is wrong, how will you know?) 
Double-check AI-generated hours, dates, posted fees, and anything involving numbers (AI is known to hallucinate!)


A note on Accessibility: Humans and AI alike are encouraged to avoid subjective assessments of accessibility such as “Wheelchair friendly” or “Easily accessible”. Instead of these vague phrases, provide detailed information about the physical description of the birding location. Include details such as trail surfaces, gradient/slope, safety features, degree of maintenance, crowd and noise levels, etc. Consider using the Birdability Site Review Checklist to guide your description. Aim to help others make an assessment for themselves whether the site is accessible to them.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
An excerpt of the parent location’s About content appears on each sub-hotspot page. Use the About sections on the parent hotspot to describe information that applies to every hotspot in the group, such as hours, fees, and stewardship details.




Features are unique to each individual hotspot. Use the Parent hotspot’s Features to indicate things that are generally true for the entire area. For example, if at least one hotspot in the group has a public bathroom, select ‘Yes’ for Restrooms on the Parent hotspot. Then select ‘Yes’ or ‘No’ on individual sub-hotspots based on whether that specific site has a bathroom.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
At the top right of the Hotspot Review Tools page, there is a “Show Reviewed” button. Click to see list of all the hotspots that have already been reviewed in your review region except for merged . This page shows who accepted or didn’t accept the hotspot and when.





All columns on this page are sortable if you click on the header—with the right two being most important. The “Reviewed On” column lets you view by time of review, and “View in eBird” takes you to the Edit hotspot screen where you can revisit hotspot decisions that have already been made.
Re-review will re-Accept a hotspot that was previously marked Not Accepted.  giving you the option to review (rename, merge, or move) it again.

title: eBird Hotspot Manual
text:
Only eBird Hotspot Editors can create and manage Hotspot groups. 



Navigate to the hotspot you want to act as the parent location

From the Hotspot map: Locate and tap the appropriate Hotspot pin, then click the Edit link (upper right corner).
From an individual Hotspot page: Tap Admin from the navigation sidebar

Tap Manage Sub-hotspots
Locate nearby hotspots on the map or use the search bar to look them up by name (another good reason to have the parent location in all sub-hotspot names!) 
Once a pin is selected on the map, tap the Add button to add that hotspot to the group
Tap the Remove button on the Sub-hotspots list to remove a hotspot from the group

You can also tap a pin on the map and, if it is already part of the group, tap Remove to remove it. 

IMPORTANT: Tap the Save Sub-hotspots button to save your changes






Follow steps 1 and 2 from Create a Hotspot Group (above) to access the Manage Sub-hotspots tab on the Parent Hotspot’s Adminpage. 
Tap Remove next to each sub-hotspot in the Sub-hotspots list
Once all sub-hotspots are removed, the hotspot will no longer function as a parent location and the group will effectively no longer exist
